endings.title: Lights Out
endings.text: The register never warmed up. Seven days of unlocking the gate, straightening the shelves, and watching the corridor stay empty was all it took for management to decide the space had better uses. Your name was gone from the directory before lunch.

endings.title: Foreclosure
endings.text: Two weeks of diminishing returns turned the storefront into a long goodbye. The regulars stopped coming, then the casual shoppers thinned, then even the hallway outside felt like it was avoiding you. The eviction notice arrived warm from the copier.

endings.title: Going, Going, Gone
endings.text: You stayed open past the point where optimism became a kind of stubborn theater. The numbers shrank slowly enough to be insulting, and the final sale felt less like commerce than sympathy. When the gate locked for the last time, the sound echoed further than it should have.

endings.title: The Prestige Champion
endings.text: Fifty thousand in revenue and a reputation that arrived in conversations before you did made your store the one every other tenant measured themselves against. Customers left satisfied, management stopped pretending not to notice, and the mall treated your unit like proof it could still produce legends. Even the stale air around the escalators felt impressed.

endings.title: The Local Legend
endings.text: One store, run well enough and long enough that it stopped being a business and started being a landmark. The food court regulars used your sign as a landmark, the parking attendants recognized your car, and people gave directions with your storefront as a reference point. That kind of reputation outlasts a lease.

endings.title: The Mall Tycoon
endings.text: Every occupied unit on the directory bore your name. Tenant meetings became a formality, the corridor became an empire, and the escalators ferried people toward your stores because there was nowhere else to go. At some point the mall stopped renting space to you and started orbiting you instead.

endings.title: The Mini Empire
endings.text: Three storefronts meant three gate codes, three sets of keys, and a daily route through the building that felt like a private transit line. The other tenants learned to watch you the way people watch weather systems: cautiously, respectfully, and with the understanding that you operated at a larger scale than they did. Control had become its own form of atmosphere.

endings.title: The Fair Dealer
endings.text: Two hundred satisfied customers walked out having paid exactly what the tag said. In a mall full of markup tricks, pressure tactics, and soft negotiations, you made fixed prices feel almost radical. People trusted your shelves the way they trusted a scale at the deli counter.

endings.title: Broke Even
endings.text: Thirty days later, the ledger balanced to a number that was technically positive and emotionally neutral. You covered the lights, paid the rent, kept the gate rolling up in the morning, and ended with enough left to call it survival. The directory still listed your store, which was the whole point of the exercise.

endings.title: The Comfortable Middle
endings.text: You found the revenue band where management stopped checking in but never started paying special attention. The rent cleared, the shelves turned over, and your store occupied the calm middle of the directory with admirable anonymity. Nothing dramatic happened, which over thirty days counted as its own kind of triumph.

endings.title: Crisis Operator
endings.text: Ten days below the bankruptcy line taught you more about timing than comfort ever could. The balance dipped, recovered, dipped again, and each rescue came at the last plausible second until nerve itself became part of the business model. By day thirty you had not escaped the edge so much as learned how to live on it.

endings.title: The Mall Between the Walls
endings.text: You started catching glimpses in the wax-buffed reflections — a directory entry that wasn't there yesterday, an escalator that descended into the same floor it began on, a gate code only you remembered using. The mall had begun keeping a private version of itself, and at some point you had walked into it and stayed. The lights came on every morning regardless of whether anyone unlocked them.

endings.title: The Mall Legend Redux
endings.text: A second 30-day run inside the same building tells a different story than the first one ever could. The regulars had names for you before you opened, the staff already knew their stations, and your inventory pricing settled into a confident dialect. By the time the second cycle ended, the legend wasn't a story being told for the first time — it was being remembered, retold, and quietly verified by everyone who had been there for the prequel.
